posts.title:
It’s exactly 10 years since my first day at Google. What I’ve learnt:

1. In 2011 there was a career path for me, but I had to leave Chile to find it.

2. There is a career path outside management:… | Felipe Hoffa | 58 comments
posts.description:
It’s exactly 10 years since my first day at Google. What I’ve learnt:

1. In 2011 there was a career path for me, but I had to leave Chile to find it.

2. There is a career path outside management: At Google I discovered that software engineers could keep moving forward without the need to become a manager.

3. There are jobs that combine passions with skills: I wasn’t the best SWE at Google, but someone saw my passions and skills, and invited me to join Developer Relations.

4. Know your strengths: Taking the Clifton StrengthsFinder test changed how I viewed myself. The results made sense, and gave me a way to communicate to others and myself who I was and what I could do.

5. Know what you want to do — If I had to summarize the 3 things that have motivated me the most throughout life it’s Travel, Storytelling, and Analyzing Data. DevRel was the perfect fit for me.

6. Choose a manager that values your strengths: You won’t always have a choice of who your next manager will be, but make sure to establish as early as possible what your strengths are and what you want to do. 

7. There is life outside Google: After almost 10 years at Google with 13 different managers, I finally had enough. I used all I had learned to find my new home at Snowflake, and it has proven to be the right choice.

8. Making mistakes is ok: I’ve made people feel uncomfortable. I’ve missed deadlines. I should have replied to more emails and messages than I have. Hopefully my contributions have been bigger than my mistakes, and I’m grateful for everyone that has forgiven me.

9. Learn from your own mistakes and shortcomings. Have empathy for those that are still learning from their own.

10. Showing up and building relationships is more important than a plan: I never planned to move to the US, join Google, nor DevRel, nor Snowflake. All these happened thanks to friends that I made along the way. I
posts.notes: Already on homepage; perennial career reflection.

posts.title:
After 3.9 years at Snowflake, it’s time for me to say goodbye and thanks.

Let me start by stating that Elise Bergeron and Denise Persson are responsible for the good things I did at Snowflake. 4… | Felipe Hoffa | 490 comments
posts.description:
After 3.9 years at Snowflake, it’s time for me to say goodbye and thanks.

Let me start by stating that Elise Bergeron and Denise Persson are responsible for the good things I did at Snowflake. 4 years ago they didn’t know me, but they worked hard to convince me to join, carved a space for me, and gave me the teammates and tools I needed to succeed. I will forever be grateful to them for this incredible opportunity, and for their continuous support throughout these 3.9 years.

Speaking of people that helped me succeed, there are so many around the world that if I tried to name them all I would quickly run out of space. So let me highlight 3, who helped me immensely throughout this journey: Aleksandra Todorova, Waqas Makhdum, and Rida Safdar. Aleksandra was my first partner on getting things done at Snowflake, giving me the inspiration and support I needed. Then I was incredibly lucky to have Waqas as my manager for most of this time. Back then our conversations led to our team hiring Rida as our first program manager. Her drive became our productivity super booster, and I can’t wait until the day we get to work together again.

As a Developer Advocate I’m lucky that much of my work is publicly visible, so you might have already seen some of it. Just this last quarter I got to host live shows on LI and YT, filmed in-depth conversations with several Powered by Snowflake partners (many still to be premiered), and presented multiple times at our Summit - including the unforgettable Builder’s Keynote demo with the notebook that failed in the middle of it.

I also want to acknowledge that I made many mistakes throughout my time at Snowflake. Turns out that’s one of the best ways to learn and grow, especially when surrounded by a supportive environment. Most of us are on a similar path, so remember to always be kind, grateful, and to forgive.

As for next steps, for 9.3 years I was “Felipe Hoffa from Google”, and then for 3.9 I became “Felipe Hoffa from Snowflake”. Now I’m eager to take some time off, and figure out who “Felipe” is. I can’t be sure, but I have a feeling I might like this guy. 

I have so much more to say, but all good things must come to an end. Let me finish this post by thanking all my friends inside and outside Snowflake — especially you. | 490 comments on LinkedIn

posts.title:
After a long break, I’ve chosen my next job. 

I’m grateful to everyone who opened a door during this search. At first, I tried to figure out who the future winners of this GenAI revolution might be.… | Felipe Hoffa | 237 comments
posts.description:
After a long break, I’ve chosen my next job. 

I’m grateful to everyone who opened a door during this search. At first, I tried to figure out who the future winners of this GenAI revolution might be. I went to countless meetups, and interviewed with cool companies. I met founders and leaders full of incredible ideas, and learned a lot. But I still couldn’t figure out who to join.

So I changed the question. Instead of wondering who will win, I asked myself 2 questions:

- “How can I become better at my job?” 

At Google, I discovered how much I love being a Developer Advocate, and that I’m pretty good at it - which led to following a similar playbook at Snowflake. But after 12 years of talking to practitioners about the tools they should use, I missed being one myself. To become a better advocate, it’s time to become a builder again.

- “How can I help improve the world?”

Silicon Valley loves the idea of "selling pickaxes during a gold rush”. But how are those pickaxes being used? As we discover what AI can do, it’s easy to predict terrible futures, but also incredible outcomes for all of us. So I started looking for opportunities to use AI for good.

That’s when my friends at Geotab called. You might not have heard of them, but Geotab is the world’s leading telematics company, tracking more than 4.7 million vehicles globally - from delivery trucks to school buses. They process billions of data points daily to make transportation safer, more efficient, and sustainable. Cities optimize traffic, logistics companies cut emissions, and drivers get home safer.

All this data and mission makes Geotab an ideal place for my talents and growth. I’m thrilled to join Geotab’s Data Science and AI team as a `Principal Data and AI Intelligence Advocate

posts.title: I’m ready to announce my newest “label”. I recently reflected on how I’m taking some time-out to figure out who “Felipe” is, leaving behind the “from Snowflake” and “from Google” labels. Well, I woke… | Felipe Hoffa | 277 comments
posts.description:
I’m ready to announce my newest “label”. I recently reflected on how I’m taking some time-out to figure out who “Felipe” is, leaving behind the “from Snowflake” and “from Google” labels. Well, I woke up this morning for a life changing interview, and a couple hours later I was sworn in as “Felipe, the new US citizen”.

Back in 2011 I was going through a similar process: I was in Chile, taking a year off-work, trying to figure out my future in the Chilean job market. I did not fit in. That’s when Google called, brought me to San Francisco, and started off a new career. I moved far away from family and friends to become who I am today. I’m incredibly grateful for how the US opened its doors and welcomed me. I could have never imagined or planned how awesome these years have been, and moving to the US made it possible.

I’d like to dedicate this milestone to another immigrant: A kid growing up in Germany before WW2. He lived with his parents and sisters as a Christian family in Kassel. One day he and his sisters were not allowed back into school. This wasn’t due to their religion, but because they had Jewish ancestors. That was enough. Germany and much of Europe rapidly became an unsafe place for them, and luckily Chile gave them the documents they needed to move. They boarded a boat from Bremen to Chile, leaving everything they had behind.

My maternal grandfather grew up in Chile, and remembered how that shy German kid arrived at his school. He told me stories of how other kids made fun of this German boy before he could speak Spanish. But that German kid got an education, grew up in Chile, started a family, a business, and so much more. He died young - I never got to meet him - but that German immigrant kid became my grandfather.

I still don’t know what comes next, but I know what my first action as a US citizen was: I registered to vote.

Sometimes there are people that push us out of the places where we are comfortable. And then there are other awesome people ready to welcome us and give us a place to thrive. Chile welcomed my grandfather, and the US has welcomed me. Thanks to everyone that made this story possible. | 277 comments on LinkedIn

posts.title:
BigQuery vs Snowflake vs Databricks: Which subreddit community beats?

Instead of comparing data platforms, I want to know which one has the healthiest community heartbeat on Reddit:  BigQuery… | Felipe Hoffa | 31 comments
posts.description:
BigQuery vs Snowflake vs Databricks: Which subreddit community beats?

Instead of comparing data platforms, I want to know which one has the healthiest community heartbeat on Reddit:  BigQuery, Snowflake, or Databricks? I’m not in the business of advocating for any of them anymore, but I’ll always love data and community. So let’s compare the numbers.

Reddit, Inc. has grown as the largest internet community, and I’ve been part of growing some subreddits close to my heart. Including:

- I started r/bigquery in 2013. I took it from 0 to 10k subscribers by 2020. After I left, it kept growing, but at a slower rate
- In 2020, I reactivated r/snowflake, taking it from 600 to 18k subscribers by 2024.
- And in 2023, I watched from outside as r/databricks went from almost nothing to 9k subscribers at the start of 2025.

So who has the healthiest subreddit community today?

This is a timely question, as Reddit just stopped reporting subscriber counts for subreddits. Instead, they’re now showing “weekly visitors” and “weekly contributors.” Not all mods are thrilled about this change, but it gives us some interesting insights — as observed on September 16, 2025:

- r/bigquery: Still the largest subscriber base (~19.6k), but surprisingly low engagement (7.5k weekly visitors, 33 weekly contributions).

- r/snowflake: Has likely matched BigQuery’s subscriber count (~18.9k) with much stronger engagement (16k weekly visitors, 172 weekly contributions)

- r/databricks: The clear engagement winner despite fewer total subscribers (20k weekly visitors, 424 weekly contributions)

What This Tells Us:

- It’s never too late to build a vibrant community: Databricks entered the game years after the others, but built the most vibrant community. Sometimes being the newcomer means you try harder.

- Communities need champions to thrive: r/bigquery’s growth slowed significantly after I left Google. I’m seeing a similar pattern with r/snowflake now that I’ve moved on. Don’t just assign someone to manage your subreddit, but nurture those who really care.

- A beating heart is more important than a large body: Raw subscriber numbers don’t tell the whole story. Databricks has developed a community where people actively participate, ask questions, and help each other.

I’m impressed by what the Databricks community team has built. My goal is to keep watching and learning from their growth.

As for me? I just revived r/Geotab for the telematics industry — a space I’m still learning about. That’s the beauty of building communities: you get to learn alongside peers, while connecting with people with shared challenges and interests.

Check the link below to find this post on Medium - with extra links and charts. | 31 comments on LinkedIn

posts.title:
7 out of 10 LLMs got this simple geographic question wrong. Can we trust AI with even basic questions?

Quick test: Which city is closer to Santiago, Chile — Los Angeles or Toronto? (Guess first, and… | Felipe Hoffa
posts.description:
7 out of 10 LLMs got this simple geographic question wrong. Can we trust AI with even basic questions?

Quick test: Which city is closer to Santiago, Chile — Los Angeles or Toronto? (Guess first, and then check the video for the answer)

The shocking part: Google Gemini, Mistral AI, OpenAI o3... all got it wrong.

Gemini even used web search, found the correct distances, and STILL flipped the answer!

Next time someone tries to win a debate citing an LLM, show them this video. When have LLMs let you down?

(check the links in the comments for the unfiltered replies from the LLMs)

posts.title:
Is MCP dead? I compared the Google Cloud Next session catalogs — 2025 vs 2026:

• MCP: 1 → 63 sessions
• A2A / agent-to-agent: 0 → 16
• "Protocol": 0 → 50

This is Google moving from “AI assistant”… | Felipe Hoffa | 10 comments
posts.description:
Is MCP dead? I compared the Google Cloud Next session catalogs — 2025 vs 2026:

• MCP: 1 → 63 sessions
• A2A / agent-to-agent: 0 → 16
• "Protocol": 0 → 50

This is Google moving from “AI assistant” framing toward something much closer to an agent execution stack. The 63 MCP sessions span 32 Breakouts, 11 Lightning Talks, 8 Discussion Groups, 5 Demos, 3 Workshops, 3 Solution Talks, 3 Birds of a Feather, 1 Spotlight, plus a Developer Meetup and a Lounge Session.

The themes are pretty consistent:

• Gemini CLI as the developer control surface
• MCP as the protocol plumbing
• ADK as the agent toolkit
• BigQuery, Apigee, Cloud Run, and SecOps as the production surfaces

There’s also a quieter shift sitting next to MCP: the word “skills": 26 sessions mention it in 2026. But the term is still in transition. Most of those sessions still mean "human training", "certifications", "upskilling programs". A smaller subset clearly means something else:

• Automating the UI with Gemini CLI, MCP and Skills
• Vibing with Gemini CLI: Custom Web AI solutions and shareable skills
• One tool to rule them all: Extending and customizing the Gemini CLI
• 10x productivity with the Gemini CLI

In those sessions, skills starts to mean reusable, executable capability — something you compose and ship, not just something a human learns in a course. The word hasn’t fully crossed over yet. But the direction is visible.

There’s also a demand signal in the reservations data: 7,778 seats already taken across the MCP sessions, weeks before the conference opens!

The six biggest registration totals alone account for well over 1,400:

• Conversational Analytics agents, API, and MCP — 356 (Richard Kuzma
• Agent development with BigQuery, ADK, and MCP — 298 (guillaume blaquiere)
• Build AI agents on Cloud Run — 256 (Ryan Pei)
• Google MCP Services — 178 (Romin Irani)
• Beyond the prompt: Build production-ready agents with Google’s MCP servers — 157 (Tarun Gumar)
• Unlocking enterprise actions: Bridge your APIs to AI agents with MCP and Apigee — 152 (David Rush)

And many of the smaller practitioner sessions are already gone (Agent development and AgentOps with BigQuery, ADK, and MCP; Building custom agents with Looker MCP and APIs; Build an AI Agent and Configure an MCP Server on Cloud Run)

Conference agendas are lagging indicators. CFPs, editorial planning, and speaker lock-in happen months before the event. So this doesn’t prove MCP wins long term. But it does show that well before the discourse settled, Google had already decided MCP was worth putting across one of its biggest stages.

And if MCP is the obvious signal, skills as executable capability may be the next term worth tracking.

→ Browse all 63 MCP sessions: https://lnkd.in/g_VCDVdv
→ Browse all 26 Skills sessions: https://lnkd.in/gGramR32
→ Read more insights https://lnkd.in/gj6HuH-E | 10 comments on LinkedIn

How does hosting the Super Bowl reshape traffic in your city? Using Altitude by Geotab’s commercial vehicle telematics (Super Bowl week vs. January baseline):

- San Francisco saw a +13% traffic… | Felipe Hoffa
posts.description:
How does hosting the Super Bowl reshape traffic in your city? Using Altitude by Geotab’s commercial vehicle telematics (Super Bowl week vs. January baseline):

- San Francisco saw a +13% traffic bump, even though the game was hosted miles away in Santa Clara.

- Santa Clara felt more than double that impact at +30% near Levi’s Stadium.

- But New Orleans during last year’s Super Bowl week dwarfed them both with a +73% increase around the Superdome.

(All numbers are for the 1.5‑mile zone around each location)

Check out the full report at the link below. Meanwhile, my own Super Bowl experience was finding a super empty SFO airport while waiting for my flight to Vegas for Geotab Connect 2026. See ya there!
